html:
XIX

It had evidently been a great festival. One of the events of Mr.¬†HancockвАЩs summer; designed by him for the happiness and enjoyment of his friends and enjoyed by him in labouring to those ends. It was beautiful to look back upon; in every part; the easy journey, the approach to the cottage along the mile of green-feathered river, the well-ordered feast in the large clean cottage; the well-thought out comfort of the cottage bedrooms, the sight of the orchard lit by Chinese lanterns, the lantern-lit boats, the drifting down the river in the soft moonlit air; the candle lit supper table, morning through the cottage windows, upstairs and down, far away from the world, people meeting at breakfast like travellers in a far-off country, pleased to see well known facesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the morning on the green riverвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the gentleness and kindliness and quiet dignity of everybody, the kindly difficult gently jesting discussion of small personal incidents; the gentle amiable strains; the mild restrained self-effacing watchfulness of the women; the uncompeting mutual admiration of the men; the general gratitude of the group when one or other of the men filled up a space of time with a piece of modestly narrated personal reminiscence.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Never, never could she belong to that world. It was a perfect little world; enclosed; something one would need to be born and trained into; the experience of it as an outsider was pure pain and misery; admiration, irritation and resentment running abreast in a fever. Welcome and kindliness could do nothing; oneвАЩs own straining towards it, nothing; a night of sleepless battering at its closed doors, nothing. There was a secret in it, in spite of its simple seeming exterior; an undesired secret. Something to which one could not give oneself up. Its terms were terms on which one could not live. That girl could live on them, in spite of her strenuous different life in the east-end settlementвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ in spite of her plain dull dress and red hands. She knew the code; her cheap straw hat waved graciously, her hair ruffled about her head in soft clouds. Why had he never spoken of her uncleвАЩs cottage so near his own? She must be always there. When she appeared in the surgery she seemed to come straight out of the east-endвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ his respect for workers amongst the poorвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ his general mild revulsion from philanthropists; but down here she was not a philanthropistвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ outwardly a girl with blowy hair and a wavy hat, smiling in boats, understanding botany and fishingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ inwardly a designing female, her mind lit by her cold intellectual вАЬethicalвАЭвБ†вАФhoooooвБ†вАФthe very sound of the wordвБ†вАФвАЬethical PantheismвАЭ; cool and secret and hateful. вАЬRather a nice little thingвАЭ; вАЬpretty green dressвАЭ; nice!
